title: Sepsis — ICU Management
oneLiner: Life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated host response to infection; requires early recognition, source control, and ICU-level support.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic vignette:** A 72-year-old man with diabetes mellitus and COPD presents from a nursing home with two days of cough, purulent sputum, and fever. On the ward he appears drowsy, his respiratory rate is 28/min, heart rate 112 bpm, BP 88/52 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 88% on room air. He has mottled skin and oliguria (<20 mL/hour).
- **Dissect the clues:**
  - *Drowsiness / confusion* → CNS hypoperfusion or direct endotoxin effect – altered mental status is a hallmark of organ dysfunction (qSOFA ≥2).
  - *Tachypnoea + hypoxia* → compensatory respiratory alkalosis plus possible pneumonia-induced shunt; a sign of both infection and tissue oxygen deficit.
  - *Tachycardia + hypotension* → vasodilatory (distributive) shock from cytokine-induced vasodilation and relative hypovolaemia.
  - *Mottled skin* → peripheral vasoconstriction in late “cold shock” or capillary leak; a sign of poor perfusion.
  - *Oliguria* → renal hypoperfusion; early acute kidney injury (AKI) from reduced renal blood flow.
  - **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
    - *The hypothermic septic patient*: elderly, immunocompromised, or on beta-blockers may present with temp <36°C (no fever). Don’t be falsely reassured – hypothermia correlates with higher mortality.
    - *The “pain-only” sepsis*: young, otherwise fit patient with severe abdominal pain from perforated diverticulitis; no obvious fever, BP normal (compensated shock). Lactate may be the only clue.
    - *The post-operative septic patient*: minimal signs because of residual anaesthesia, opioids, or drains. Look for unexplained hypotension, new confusion, rising lactate or white cell count.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- **Plain-language definition:** Sepsis is your body’s extreme, damaging response to an infection. Instead of just fighting the bug, the immune system goes into overdrive, causing inflammation and blood flow problems that injure your own organs. When blood pressure drops despite fluids and organ failure worsens, it becomes septic shock.
- **Why it matters clinically:**
  - Sepsis is the leading cause of ICU admission and in-hospital mortality worldwide – 1 in 5 deaths globally is sepsis-related.
  - It is time-critical: the risk of death increases by ~8% for every hour of delayed antibiotics.
  - Common pitfall: **failure to recognise compensated shock** (normal BP but high lactate, tachycardia, low urine output). The classic “septic shock” with hypotension is already late.
  - Early, structured management reduces mortality by >20%.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
The story begins with a pathogen (e.g., Gram-negative bacteria) entering the bloodstream or tissue. Pattern-recognition receptors (TLRs) on immune cells detect the invader and trigger a massive release of **pro-inflammatory cytokines** (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6).

- **Cytokine storm → vasodilation** (via nitric oxide) → decreased systemic vascular resistance → **hypotension** (the distributive shock pattern).
- **Capillary leak** (endothelial injury) → intravascular fluid shifts into interstitium → relative hypovolaemia and oedema → further reduces BP and tissue perfusion.
- **Microvascular thrombosis** (coagulation cascade activation) → DIC → worsens organ ischemia.
- **Mitochondrial dysfunction** → cells can’t use oxygen even if delivered → **lactate production** (Type A + Type B) → elevated serum lactate.
- **Baroreflex activation** → tachycardia (compensatory) in early shock.
- **CNS hypoperfusion + direct cytokine effect** → acute encephalopathy, delirium, drowsiness.

| Clinical Sign | Pathophysiological Mechanism |
|---------------|------------------------------|
| Fever / hypothermia | Hypothalamic reset by pyrogens (IL-1, TNF) |
| Tachypnoea | Compensatory respiratory alkalosis; direct effect of acidosis on respiratory centre |
| Tachycardia | Baroreceptor response to ↓SVR; catecholamine surge |
| Hypotension | ↓SVR from vasodilation; ↓preload from capillary leak |
| Confusion | ↓CBF, local inflammation in brain, metabolic derangement |
| Lactate >2 mmol/L | Anaerobic metabolism (poor perfusion) + mitochondrial dysfunction |
| Oliguria | ↓Renal perfusion pressure; prerenal AKI (can progress to intrinsic if prolonged) |

This pathophysiological story explains the pattern recognition cues: the initial “warm shock” (bounding pulses, pink skin) is the vasodilation phase; later “cold shock” (mottled, shut-down) is when compensatory vasoconstriction takes over, often indicating worsening perfusion.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
**Most common cause:** Community-acquired pneumonia (Streptococcus pneumoniae, influenza, COVID-19 superinfection).

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Bacterial – Gram-negative** | E. coli pyelonephritis, Klebsiella pneumonia, Pseudomonas in neutropenia | “Febrile, flank pain and dysuria” or “hospital-acquired pneumonia” |
| **Bacterial – Gram-positive** | Staph aureus (skin, IV line), Strep pyogenes (necrotising fasciitis) | “Cellulitis + rapid hypotensive crisis” or “surgical wound infection” |
| **Bacterial – intra-abdominal** | Perforated viscus (diverticulitis, appendicitis), biliary sepsis | “Severe abdominal pain, guarding, jaundice” |
| **Viral** | Influenza, SARS-CoV-2 | “Cough, myalgia, rapid COVID PCR positive” – watch for cytokine storm |
| **Fungal** | Candida spp (ICU, post-abdominal surgery) | “Persistent fever despite broad-spectrum antibiotics, central line” |
| **Parasitic / rickettsial** | Malaria, typhus | “Travel history, cyclical fevers, headache” |
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (Sepsis-3):** Organ dysfunction = increase in SOFA score ≥2 points (acutely) in context of infection. For ward screening: **qSOFA** (≥2: RR≥22, altered mentation, SBP≤100). qSOFA is not diagnostic but high-risk.

| Test | What it shows | Why order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Lactate** | Tissue hypoperfusion | Screen for shock; initial & serial | >2 mmol/L = tissue hypoperfusion; >4 = septic shock (with hypotension after fluids) |
| **Blood cultures (2 sets)** | Circulating pathogen | Identify source & antibiotic sensitivity | Positive = septic source; negative does not rule out – many culture-negative |
| **FBC** | WCC, platelets, Hb | Infection, DIC | Leucocytosis/leucopaenia; thrombocytopenia clues for DIC |
| **CRP / Procalcitonin** | Inflammation / bacterial infection | Trend for response | Procalcitonin >0.5 µg/L supports bacterial aetiology; decreasing levels guide antibiotic de-escalation |
| **ABG** | pH, PaO2, PaCO2, HCO3-, lactate | Acidosis, oxygenation, respiratory compensation | Metabolic acidosis with high lactate; elevated PaCO2 may indicate hypoventilation |
| **Renal function + LFTs** | AKI, hepatic injury | Baseline and trend for organ failure | Early AKI: ↑creatinine, oliguria; ↑bilirubin = hepatic dysfunction (sepsis cholestasis) |
| **Coagulation screen** | PT, APTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer | DIC assessment | ↑PT/APTT, ↓fibrinogen, ↑D-dimer → overt DIC |
| **Chest X-ray / CT** | Pneumonia, abscess | Source identification | Focal infiltrate → pneumonia; free air → perforation |

**Pattern recognition:** The single finding that *confirms* sepsis is **organ dysfunction in the setting of infection** (any one of lactate >2, altered mental state, hypotension, oliguria, raised creatinine, thrombocytopenia). No single test rules it out – a negative culture doesn’t rule out sepsis.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – the “Golden Hour”**
- **ABCDE approach** – stabilise airway, breathing, circulation.
- **Oxygen** to target SpO2 ≥94% (caution in COPD – aim 88-92%).
- **IV fluids**: Crystalloid (balanced solution like Ringer’s lactate is preferred over normal saline to avoid hyperchloraemic acidosis) – give 30 mL/kg ideal body weight over the first 3 hours if hypotensive or lactate >4. *Why*: capillary leak reduces intravascular volume; restoring preload improves cardiac output.
- **Blood cultures** (do not delay antibiotics for cultures if septic shock – take cultures on the way to giving antibiotics).
- **Antibiotics** within 1 hour: broad-spectrum, e.g., Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h. If septic shock: add gentamicin 5-7 mg/kg IV once (renal function adjusted). If necrotising soft tissue infection: add clindamycin 900 mg q8h.
- **Measure lactate** – repeat in 2-4 hours to assess response (lactate clearance >20% predicts better outcome).
- **Central line** if vasopressors needed; **arterial line** for continuous BP.

**Ward / ICU (24-72 hours)**
- **Reassess volume status** – dynamic measures: passive leg raise, pulse pressure variation, echocardiography. Avoid excessive fluids if no longer responsive; switch to vasopressors.
- **Vasopressors** if fluid-refractory: **norepinephrine** – start at 0.05-0.1 µg/kg/min, titrate to MAP ≥65 mmHg. If norepinephrine alone insufficient: add **vasopressin** 0.03 IU/min or **epinephrine** (0.02-0.1 µg/kg/min).
- **Source control** – drain abscess, remove infected lines, surgery for perforation/necrosis. *Every hour of delay increases mortality.*
- **De-escalate antibiotics** – stop empirical coverage once cultures and sensitivities return (e.g., 48h). Procalcitonin-guided cessation can reduce duration.
- **Organ support**: mechanical ventilation (lung protective, low tidal volumes 6 mL/kg predicted body weight, plateau pressure ≤30 cmH2O); renal replacement therapy (CRRT or IHD) for AKI with hyperkalaemia, acidosis, uraemia, fluid overload.
- **Glucose control** – target 7.8-10 mmol/L; avoid hypo- and hyperglycaemia.

**Long-term**
- **Treat the underlying infection** – complete appropriate course.
- **Rehabilitation** – ICU-acquired weakness (early physiotherapy, mobilisation); cognitive impairment.
- **Steroids?** – Consider hydrocortisone (200 mg/day IV in divided doses) only in septic shock refractory to vasopressors despite adequate fluids. Do not give routinely – recent trials show no mortality benefit outside shock.
- **What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
  - ❌ Delaying antibiotics to wait for cultures in unstable patient – leads to death.
  - ❌ Giving 30 mL/kg fluid bolus to someone with known severe LV failure without non-invasive monitoring (can cause pulmonary oedema). Use smaller boluses and assess response.
  - ❌ Using dobutamine for hypotension (dobutamine is an inotrope, not a vasopressor; it can worsen hypotension if SVR is very low).
  - ❌ Keeping the patient on the same empirical antibiotics for >72h if cultures are negative and no source found – consider stopping or narrowing after careful review.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Septic shock | Cardiogenic shock | Haemorrhagic shock | Anaphylactic shock |
|---------|--------------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Pain** | Variable (localised to infection site) | Chest pain (MI) or no pain (pump failure) | Trauma or abdominal pain | Usually none; may have throat tightness, urticaria |
| **Exam** | Fever/hypothermia, warm or cold peripheries, hyperdynamic (bounding) in early stage | Cool clammy extremities, pulmonary oedema, JVD ↑, S3 gallop | Pale, cool, diaphoretic, often obvious haemorrhage site (external or concealed) | Urticaria, angioedema, wheeze, stridor, hypotension with or without tachycardia |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia; non-specific ST changes from demand ischaemia | STEMI, wide QRS, ST depression; LV strain | Sinus tachycardia | Sinus tachycardia; may have Brugada-like pattern (rare) |
| **Labs** | ↑Lactate, ↑procalcitonin, ↑WCC/thrombocytopenia, coagulopathy | ↑Troponin, ↑BNP, ↑lactate (if severe), possible new ECG changes | ↓Hb, ↓Hct, ↑lactate, may have ↑INR (from dilution/coagulopathy) | ↑Lactate (late), ↑tryptase, ↑histamine; normal Hb and cultures |
| **Key differentiator** | Source of infection (cough, dysuria, cellulitis) + positive cultures + fever + response to fluids + vasopressors | Severe LV dysfunction on echo, high filling pressures, responds better to inotropes+vasodilators | Clear bleeding source, low CVP, low Hb, responds to blood products + surgery | Allergen exposure, urticaria, angioedema, responds to adrenaline |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Septic shock with refractory hypotension** = persistent MAP <65 despite adequate fluids and high-dose norepinephrine → multiorgan dysfunction, death. *What you'll see*: cold, mottled, confused, anuric. **What to do**: add vasopressin, consider echocardiography to exclude occult cardiomyopathy; start hydrocortisone.
- **Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)** – develops 12-48h after onset. *Pattern*: worsening dyspnoea, bilateral chest X-ray opacities, PaO2/FiO2 <300. Requires lung-protective ventilation, prone positioning, careful fluid management.
- **Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)** – prerenal -> intrinsic (ATN). Anuria, rising creatinine, hyperkalaemia. Needs RRT if refractory.
- **Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)** – widespread microthrombi + bleeding. *Signs*: petechiae, bruising, bleeding from venepuncture sites, ↓platelets, ↑PT/APTT, ↓fibrinogen. Treat underlying sepsis; consider platelet transfusion if bleeding and platelets <20-30, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.5 g/L.
- **Long-term sequelae** – ICU-acquired weakness, cognitive decline, post-sepsis syndrome (fatigue, depression). Early physio and follow-up are essential.

**Late presentation (the “missed sepsis” stem):** A patient presents from a nursing home with hypotension, anuria, confusion, and bilateral crackles on CXR. Lactate is 8, INR is 2.5, platelets 40k. They never received early antibiotics. This is end-stage septic shock with DIC, ARDS, and AKI. Immediate intubation, central lines, vasopressors, RRT, and broad-spectrum antibiotics – but mortality is >80%.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
- **SEPSIS** → Source control, Early antibiotics, Pressure optimization (fluids/vasopressors), Support organ failure, Identify (cultures), Serial lactate.
- **SURVIVE** – for resuscitation: **S**ource, **U**ltrasound (echo/IVC), **R**esuscitate (fluids + vasopressors), **V**entilate, **I**ndicator (lactate clearance), **V**asopressin if needed, **E**valuate response.

**Clinical Pearls:**
1. **Lactate is your best friend** – any value >2 mmol/L in a septic patient is a red flag; >4 is septic shock (even with normal BP). Serial lactate clearance (>20% over 2-4h) predicts better outcome.
2. **Blood cultures before antibiotics?** – Yes, but if septic shock, you have 5-10 minutes; do not delay ABx beyond 1 hour. Take cultures on the way to giving the dose.
3. **Avoid excessive crystalloid** – after the initial 30 mL/kg, dynamic parameters (passive leg raise, PPV, echo) are better than central venous pressure. Over-resuscitation worsens ARDS and AKI.
4. **Source control is the definitive treatment** – a drainable collection, necrotic tissue, or obstructed bile duct will not resolve with antibiotics alone. Escalate to surgeon/interventional radiology early.
5. **Think adrenal insufficiency** – if a patient with septic shock requires very high dose vasopressors (norepinephrine >0.5 µg/kg/min) and has a history of chronic steroid use, Addison’s, or pituitary disease → give stress-dose hydrocortisone (50 mg IV q6h).
6. **The “normotensive septic” is a trap** – compensated shock can have a normal BP but high lactate, tachycardia, poor cap refill (>2 sec). This patient is about to decompensate. Act as aggressively as for hypotensive sepsis.
7. **Antibiotic timing is everything** – a 30-minute delay in hypotension can double mortality. Have a “sepsis box” ready with piperacillin-tazobactam and gentamicin.

**Common ward mistakes to avoid:**
- ❌ Calling a septic patient “anxiety” or “panic attack” because of hyperventilation and tachycardia, missing the fever and high lactate.
- ❌ Giving 500 mL NS and waiting – the initial bolus must be 30 mL/kg rapidly (over 1-2 hours).
- ❌ Forgetting to check procalcitonin or blood cultures before antibiotics in a stable patient with no hypotension.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior/ICU team (immediate):**
- NEWS2 **≥7** (or >5 with any single parameter red score)
- Lactate **>4 mmol/L** (even if BP normal)
- Persistent hypotension (SBP <90 or MAP <65) despite **30 mL/kg crystalloid** bolus
- Need for vasopressors (norepinephrine)
- GCS drop **>2 points** (or acute confusion)
- Respiratory failure – SpO2 <90%, PaO2 <8 kPa on ≥60% O2, or tachypnoea >30/min
- Oliguria **<0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 hours** despite fluids
- Concern for **source requiring urgent drainage** (abscess, perforation, necrotising fasciitis)

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2** ≥5 in any single parameter (resp rate ≥25, sat ≤91%, SBP ≤90) → immediate doctor review.
- **Lactate** >2 mmol/L triggers repeat measurement and senior review.
- **qSOFA** ≥2 in any ward patient should prompt workup for sepsis and consideration of ICU step-up.

**When to call for ICU bed:**
- Any patient requiring **vasopressors** (norepinephrine) for >2 hours despite source control
- **Lactate** >4 mmol/L not clearing within 2 hours
- **AKI** requiring renal replacement therapy
- **Respiratory failure** requiring non-invasive ventilation or intubation
- **Multi-organ failure** (≥2 organ systems)

In summary, sepsis is a disease of time. The junior doctor’s role is to recognise it, start fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately, get cultures, and escalate without delay. Pattern recognition – the warm, tachycardic, febrile patient with a high lactate – saves lives.

rawMarkdown:
## Sepsis — ICU Management
**One-liner:** Life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated host response to infection; requires early recognition, source control, and ICU-level support.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You’ll See
- **Classic vignette:** A 72-year-old man with diabetes mellitus and COPD presents from a nursing home with two days of cough, purulent sputum, and fever. On the ward he appears drowsy, his respiratory rate is 28/min, heart rate 112 bpm, BP 88/52 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 88% on room air. He has mottled skin and oliguria (<20 mL/hour).
- **Dissect the clues:**
  - *Drowsiness / confusion* → CNS hypoperfusion or direct endotoxin effect – altered mental status is a hallmark of organ dysfunction (qSOFA ≥2).
  - *Tachypnoea + hypoxia* → compensatory respiratory alkalosis plus possible pneumonia-induced shunt; a sign of both infection and tissue oxygen deficit.
  - *Tachycardia + hypotension* → vasodilatory (distributive) shock from cytokine-induced vasodilation and relative hypovolaemia.
  - *Mottled skin* → peripheral vasoconstriction in late “cold shock” or capillary leak; a sign of poor perfusion.
  - *Oliguria* → renal hypoperfusion; early acute kidney injury (AKI) from reduced renal blood flow.
  - **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
    - *The hypothermic septic patient*: elderly, immunocompromised, or on beta-blockers may present with temp <36°C (no fever). Don’t be falsely reassured – hypothermia correlates with higher mortality.
    - *The “pain-only” sepsis*: young, otherwise fit patient with severe abdominal pain from perforated diverticulitis; no obvious fever, BP normal (compensated shock). Lactate may be the only clue.
    - *The post-operative septic patient*: minimal signs because of residual anaesthesia, opioids, or drains. Look for unexplained hypotension, new confusion, rising lactate or white cell count.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
- **Plain-language definition:** Sepsis is your body’s extreme, damaging response to an infection. Instead of just fighting the bug, the immune system goes into overdrive, causing inflammation and blood flow problems that injure your own organs. When blood pressure drops despite fluids and organ failure worsens, it becomes septic shock.
- **Why it matters clinically:**
  - Sepsis is the leading cause of ICU admission and in-hospital mortality worldwide – 1 in 5 deaths globally is sepsis-related.
  - It is time-critical: the risk of death increases by ~8% for every hour of delayed antibiotics.
  - Common pitfall: **failure to recognise compensated shock** (normal BP but high lactate, tachycardia, low urine output). The classic “septic shock” with hypotension is already late.
  - Early, structured management reduces mortality by >20%.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings
The story begins with a pathogen (e.g., Gram-negative bacteria) entering the bloodstream or tissue. Pattern-recognition receptors (TLRs) on immune cells detect the invader and trigger a massive release of **pro-inflammatory cytokines** (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6).

- **Cytokine storm → vasodilation** (via nitric oxide) → decreased systemic vascular resistance → **hypotension** (the distributive shock pattern).
- **Capillary leak** (endothelial injury) → intravascular fluid shifts into interstitium → relative hypovolaemia and oedema → further reduces BP and tissue perfusion.
- **Microvascular thrombosis** (coagulation cascade activation) → DIC → worsens organ ischemia.
- **Mitochondrial dysfunction** → cells can’t use oxygen even if delivered → **lactate production** (Type A + Type B) → elevated serum lactate.
- **Baroreflex activation** → tachycardia (compensatory) in early shock.
- **CNS hypoperfusion + direct cytokine effect** → acute encephalopathy, delirium, drowsiness.

| Clinical Sign | Pathophysiological Mechanism |
|---------------|------------------------------|
| Fever / hypothermia | Hypothalamic reset by pyrogens (IL-1, TNF) |
| Tachypnoea | Compensatory respiratory alkalosis; direct effect of acidosis on respiratory centre |
| Tachycardia | Baroreceptor response to ↓SVR; catecholamine surge |
| Hypotension | ↓SVR from vasodilation; ↓preload from capillary leak |
| Confusion | ↓CBF, local inflammation in brain, metabolic derangement |
| Lactate >2 mmol/L | Anaerobic metabolism (poor perfusion) + mitochondrial dysfunction |
| Oliguria | ↓Renal perfusion pressure; prerenal AKI (can progress to intrinsic if prolonged) |

This pathophysiological story explains the pattern recognition cues: the initial “warm shock” (bounding pulses, pink skin) is the vasodilation phase; later “cold shock” (mottled, shut-down) is when compensatory vasoconstriction takes over, often indicating worsening perfusion.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)
**Most common cause:** Community-acquired pneumonia (Streptococcus pneumoniae, influenza, COVID-19 superinfection).

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Bacterial – Gram-negative** | E. coli pyelonephritis, Klebsiella pneumonia, Pseudomonas in neutropenia | “Febrile, flank pain and dysuria” or “hospital-acquired pneumonia” |
| **Bacterial – Gram-positive** | Staph aureus (skin, IV line), Strep pyogenes (necrotising fasciitis) | “Cellulitis + rapid hypotensive crisis” or “surgical wound infection” |
| **Bacterial – intra-abdominal** | Perforated viscus (diverticulitis, appendicitis), biliary sepsis | “Severe abdominal pain, guarding, jaundice” |
| **Viral** | Influenza, SARS-CoV-2 | “Cough, myalgia, rapid COVID PCR positive” – watch for cytokine storm |
| **Fungal** | Candida spp (ICU, post-abdominal surgery) | “Persistent fever despite broad-spectrum antibiotics, central line” |
| **Parasitic / rickettsial** | Malaria, typhus | “Travel history, cyclical fevers, headache” |

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria (Sepsis-3):** Organ dysfunction = increase in SOFA score ≥2 points (acutely) in context of infection. For ward screening: **qSOFA** (≥2: RR≥22, altered mentation, SBP≤100). qSOFA is not diagnostic but high-risk.

| Test | What it shows | Why order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Lactate** | Tissue hypoperfusion | Screen for shock; initial & serial | >2 mmol/L = tissue hypoperfusion; >4 = septic shock (with hypotension after fluids) |
| **Blood cultures (2 sets)** | Circulating pathogen | Identify source & antibiotic sensitivity | Positive = septic source; negative does not rule out – many culture-negative |
| **FBC** | WCC, platelets, Hb | Infection, DIC | Leucocytosis/leucopaenia; thrombocytopenia clues for DIC |
| **CRP / Procalcitonin** | Inflammation / bacterial infection | Trend for response | Procalcitonin >0.5 µg/L supports bacterial aetiology; decreasing levels guide antibiotic de-escalation |
| **ABG** | pH, PaO2, PaCO2, HCO3-, lactate | Acidosis, oxygenation, respiratory compensation | Metabolic acidosis with high lactate; elevated PaCO2 may indicate hypoventilation |
| **Renal function + LFTs** | AKI, hepatic injury | Baseline and trend for organ failure | Early AKI: ↑creatinine, oliguria; ↑bilirubin = hepatic dysfunction (sepsis cholestasis) |
| **Coagulation screen** | PT, APTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer | DIC assessment | ↑PT/APTT, ↓fibrinogen, ↑D-dimer → overt DIC |
| **Chest X-ray / CT** | Pneumonia, abscess | Source identification | Focal infiltrate → pneumonia; free air → perforation |

**Pattern recognition:** The single finding that *confirms* sepsis is **organ dysfunction in the setting of infection** (any one of lactate >2, altered mental state, hypotension, oliguria, raised creatinine, thrombocytopenia). No single test rules it out – a negative culture doesn’t rule out sepsis.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour) – the “Golden Hour”**
- **ABCDE approach** – stabilise airway, breathing, circulation.
- **Oxygen** to target SpO2 ≥94% (caution in COPD – aim 88-92%).
- **IV fluids**: Crystalloid (balanced solution like Ringer’s lactate is preferred over normal saline to avoid hyperchloraemic acidosis) – give 30 mL/kg ideal body weight over the first 3 hours if hypotensive or lactate >4. *Why*: capillary leak reduces intravascular volume; restoring preload improves cardiac output.
- **Blood cultures** (do not delay antibiotics for cultures if septic shock – take cultures on the way to giving antibiotics).
- **Antibiotics** within 1 hour: broad-spectrum, e.g., Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h. If septic shock: add gentamicin 5-7 mg/kg IV once (renal function adjusted). If necrotising soft tissue infection: add clindamycin 900 mg q8h.
- **Measure lactate** – repeat in 2-4 hours to assess response (lactate clearance >20% predicts better outcome).
- **Central line** if vasopressors needed; **arterial line** for continuous BP.

**Ward / ICU (24-72 hours)**
- **Reassess volume status** – dynamic measures: passive leg raise, pulse pressure variation, echocardiography. Avoid excessive fluids if no longer responsive; switch to vasopressors.
- **Vasopressors** if fluid-refractory: **norepinephrine** – start at 0.05-0.1 µg/kg/min, titrate to MAP ≥65 mmHg. If norepinephrine alone insufficient: add **vasopressin** 0.03 IU/min or **epinephrine** (0.02-0.1 µg/kg/min).
- **Source control** – drain abscess, remove infected lines, surgery for perforation/necrosis. *Every hour of delay increases mortality.*
- **De-escalate antibiotics** – stop empirical coverage once cultures and sensitivities return (e.g., 48h). Procalcitonin-guided cessation can reduce duration.
- **Organ support**: mechanical ventilation (lung protective, low tidal volumes 6 mL/kg predicted body weight, plateau pressure ≤30 cmH2O); renal replacement therapy (CRRT or IHD) for AKI with hyperkalaemia, acidosis, uraemia, fluid overload.
- **Glucose control** – target 7.8-10 mmol/L; avoid hypo- and hyperglycaemia.

**Long-term**
- **Treat the underlying infection** – complete appropriate course.
- **Rehabilitation** – ICU-acquired weakness (early physiotherapy, mobilisation); cognitive impairment.
- **Steroids?** – Consider hydrocortisone (200 mg/day IV in divided doses) only in septic shock refractory to vasopressors despite adequate fluids. Do not give routinely – recent trials show no mortality benefit outside shock.
- **What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
  - ❌ Delaying antibiotics to wait for cultures in unstable patient – leads to death.
  - ❌ Giving 30 mL/kg fluid bolus to someone with known severe LV failure without non-invasive monitoring (can cause pulmonary oedema). Use smaller boluses and assess response.
  - ❌ Using dobutamine for hypotension (dobutamine is an inotrope, not a vasopressor; it can worsen hypotension if SVR is very low).
  - ❌ Keeping the patient on the same empirical antibiotics for >72h if cultures are negative and no source found – consider stopping or narrowing after careful review.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
| Feature | Septic shock | Cardiogenic shock | Haemorrhagic shock | Anaphylactic shock |
|---------|--------------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Pain** | Variable (localised to infection site) | Chest pain (MI) or no pain (pump failure) | Trauma or abdominal pain | Usually none; may have throat tightness, urticaria |
| **Exam** | Fever/hypothermia, warm or cold peripheries, hyperdynamic (bounding) in early stage | Cool clammy extremities, pulmonary oedema, JVD ↑, S3 gallop | Pale, cool, diaphoretic, often obvious haemorrhage site (external or concealed) | Urticaria, angioedema, wheeze, stridor, hypotension with or without tachycardia |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia; non-specific ST changes from demand ischaemia | STEMI, wide QRS, ST depression; LV strain | Sinus tachycardia | Sinus tachycardia; may have Brugada-like pattern (rare) |
| **Labs** | ↑Lactate, ↑procalcitonin, ↑WCC/thrombocytopenia, coagulopathy | ↑Troponin, ↑BNP, ↑lactate (if severe), possible new ECG changes | ↓Hb, ↓Hct, ↑lactate, may have ↑INR (from dilution/coagulopathy) | ↑Lactate (late), ↑tryptase, ↑histamine; normal Hb and cultures |
| **Key differentiator** | Source of infection (cough, dysuria, cellulitis) + positive cultures + fever + response to fluids + vasopressors | Severe LV dysfunction on echo, high filling pressures, responds better to inotropes+vasodilators | Clear bleeding source, low CVP, low Hb, responds to blood products + surgery | Allergen exposure, urticaria, angioedema, responds to adrenaline |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
- **Septic shock with refractory hypotension** = persistent MAP <65 despite adequate fluids and high-dose norepinephrine → multiorgan dysfunction, death. *What you'll see*: cold, mottled, confused, anuric. **What to do**: add vasopressin, consider echocardiography to exclude occult cardiomyopathy; start hydrocortisone.
- **Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)** – develops 12-48h after onset. *Pattern*: worsening dyspnoea, bilateral chest X-ray opacities, PaO2/FiO2 <300. Requires lung-protective ventilation, prone positioning, careful fluid management.
- **Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)** – prerenal -> intrinsic (ATN). Anuria, rising creatinine, hyperkalaemia. Needs RRT if refractory.
- **Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)** – widespread microthrombi + bleeding. *Signs*: petechiae, bruising, bleeding from venepuncture sites, ↓platelets, ↑PT/APTT, ↓fibrinogen. Treat underlying sepsis; consider platelet transfusion if bleeding and platelets <20-30, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.5 g/L.
- **Long-term sequelae** – ICU-acquired weakness, cognitive decline, post-sepsis syndrome (fatigue, depression). Early physio and follow-up are essential.

**Late presentation (the “missed sepsis” stem):** A patient presents from a nursing home with hypotension, anuria, confusion, and bilateral crackles on CXR. Lactate is 8, INR is 2.5, platelets 40k. They never received early antibiotics. This is end-stage septic shock with DIC, ARDS, and AKI. Immediate intubation, central lines, vasopressors, RRT, and broad-spectrum antibiotics – but mortality is >80%.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**
- **SEPSIS** → Source control, Early antibiotics, Pressure optimization (fluids/vasopressors), Support organ failure, Identify (cultures), Serial lactate.
- **SURVIVE** – for resuscitation: **S**ource, **U**ltrasound (echo/IVC), **R**esuscitate (fluids + vasopressors), **V**entilate, **I**ndicator (lactate clearance), **V**asopressin if needed, **E**valuate response.

**Clinical Pearls:**
1. **Lactate is your best friend** – any value >2 mmol/L in a septic patient is a red flag; >4 is septic shock (even with normal BP). Serial lactate clearance (>20% over 2-4h) predicts better outcome.
2. **Blood cultures before antibiotics?** – Yes, but if septic shock, you have 5-10 minutes; do not delay ABx beyond 1 hour. Take cultures on the way to giving the dose.
3. **Avoid excessive crystalloid** – after the initial 30 mL/kg, dynamic parameters (passive leg raise, PPV, echo) are better than central venous pressure. Over-resuscitation worsens ARDS and AKI.
4. **Source control is the definitive treatment** – a drainable collection, necrotic tissue, or obstructed bile duct will not resolve with antibiotics alone. Escalate to surgeon/interventional radiology early.
5. **Think adrenal insufficiency** – if a patient with septic shock requires very high dose vasopressors (norepinephrine >0.5 µg/kg/min) and has a history of chronic steroid use, Addison’s, or pituitary disease → give stress-dose hydrocortisone (50 mg IV q6h).
6. **The “normotensive septic” is a trap** – compensated shock can have a normal BP but high lactate, tachycardia, poor cap refill (>2 sec). This patient is about to decompensate. Act as aggressively as for hypotensive sepsis.
7. **Antibiotic timing is everything** – a 30-minute delay in hypotension can double mortality. Have a “sepsis box” ready with piperacillin-tazobactam and gentamicin.

**Common ward mistakes to avoid:**
- ❌ Calling a septic patient “anxiety” or “panic attack” because of hyperventilation and tachycardia, missing the fever and high lactate.
- ❌ Giving 500 mL NS and waiting – the initial bolus must be 30 mL/kg rapidly (over 1-2 hours).
- ❌ Forgetting to check procalcitonin or blood cultures before antibiotics in a stable patient with no hypotension.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call senior/ICU team (immediate):**
- NEWS2 **≥7** (or >5 with any single parameter red score)
- Lactate **>4 mmol/L** (even if BP normal)
- Persistent hypotension (SBP <90 or MAP <65) despite **30 mL/kg crystalloid** bolus
- Need for vasopressors (norepinephrine)
- GCS drop **>2 points** (or acute confusion)
- Respiratory failure – SpO2 <90%, PaO2 <8 kPa on ≥60% O2, or tachypnoea >30/min
- Oliguria **<0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 hours** despite fluids
- Concern for **source requiring urgent drainage** (abscess, perforation, necrotising fasciitis)

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2** ≥5 in any single parameter (resp rate ≥25, sat ≤91%, SBP ≤90) → immediate doctor review.
- **Lactate** >2 mmol/L triggers repeat measurement and senior review.
- **qSOFA** ≥2 in any ward patient should prompt workup for sepsis and consideration of ICU step-up.

**When to call for ICU bed:**
- Any patient requiring **vasopressors** (norepinephrine) for >2 hours despite source control
- **Lactate** >4 mmol/L not clearing within 2 hours
- **AKI** requiring renal replacement therapy
- **Respiratory failure** requiring non-invasive ventilation or intubation
- **Multi-organ failure** (≥2 organ systems)

In summary, sepsis is a disease of time. The junior doctor’s role is to recognise it, start fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately, get cultures, and escalate without delay. Pattern recognition – the warm, tachycardic, febrile patient with a high lactate – saves lives.
